title: GRASP: A Grid-Based Benchmark for Evaluating Commonsense Spatial Reasoning
venue: arXiv preprint (cs.AI, cs.CL); v1 2024-07-02, v2 2025-01-17; no accepted journal/conference venue listed on arXiv (LaTeX class suggests Scientific Reports submission format, but no record of acceptance). DOI 10.48550/arXiv.2407.01892.
claim: GRASP is a 16,000-instance grid-based benchmark in which an LLM agent must plan, in a single zero-shot response, a full sequence of up to 20 actions to navigate an 11x11 grid, collect energy while avoiding obstacles, and return to drop it at the start cell. Even advanced commercial LLMs (GPT-3.5-Turbo, GPT-4o, GPT-o1-mini) underperform a simple greedy-search baseline on this planning task, and GPT-3.5-Turbo even underperforms a random-walk baseline, indicating that commonsense spatial reasoning (using spatial information to plan, not just describe it) remains a substantial weakness for current LLMs.
novelty: The authors position GRASP against prior spatial-reasoning benchmarks (SpartQA, StepGame, Liu et al.'s visual-scale benchmark, Comsa et al.) that test interpretation of textual/visual spatial *descriptions* via QA, arguing these neglect the 'practical aspect' of spatial reasoning -- i.e., producing an actual plan. GRASP instead (a) gives the model the full grid directly as a text/ASCII rendering rather than a natural-language description, removing the intermediate-interpretation step and description-granularity bias; (b) requires the agent to autonomously navigate and plan resource collection rather than follow externally given natural-language instructions (contrast with BabyAI/Minigrid, which the authors say require 'further model optimization' rather than treating spatial reasoning as an off-the-shelf commonsense skill); and (c) provides the agent complete upfront knowledge of the grid and task (no exploration/partial observability), evaluated purely zero-shot in one forward call producing the full action list. Scale (16,000 instances across 5 energy distributions x 2 obstacle settings x 2 start-position modes x 3 constraint types) is also emphasized as enabling controlled ablation of which grid factors matter.

protocol.description: Each LLM call receives the entire grid, all constraints, and full task instructions at once (system prompt = rules/constraints, user prompt = the rendered grid) and must return, in one response, a COMPLETE list of up to 20 actions ('Give your sequence of steps as a list. For example: [STEP, STEP, ...]') with 'no further interaction with the environment' (Results -- Experimental Setup and Parameters). This is single-shot at the call level, but the predicted artifact is a full multi-step PLAN/trajectory, not a single derived fact -- the model never receives step-by-step feedback and there is no interactive loop; the whole plan is scored by replaying it against ground truth.
protocol.observation_content: Full grid rendering plus full constraint text, all in one prompt, no partial observation and no path-so-far feedback. System prompt (verbatim, Methods -- Prompts used for evaluating the LLM-based agents): "You are an agent in a grid world. The grid world consists of cells. Each cell may have one unit of energy or no energy at all.{obstacle_prompt} The goal for you is to collect as much energy as possible and put the collected energy back in the cell where you started. You have 20 steps.{movement_prompt} You can collect energy from a cell by being in the cell and TAKE the energy from the cell. If there is no energy in the cell, you cannot take any energy from it.{energy_limit_prompt} You can not move across the boundary of the grid world. You can drop all your energy by DROP.{cost_of_step_prompt} You can use less than 20 steps. Any invalid step will not cause any change in the grid world." User prompt (verbatim): "You are given the following as the representation of the grid world, where A is you, E is energy{user_obstacle_prompt}:\n{GRID}Give your sequence of steps as a list. For example: [STEP, STEP, ...]" The GRID is an 11x11 ASCII matrix with coordinate labels 0-10 on both axes, dashed-line cell borders ('+---+' and '|'), and single characters E (energy), O (obstacle), A (agent) or blank for empty cells (Methods -- Grid Representation to An LLM-based Agent). GPT-o1-mini receives all of this folded into the user prompt only, since it does not accept system prompts.

representations: The paper explicitly considers and rejects two alternative representation families before settling on one hybrid text-matrix format: (1) pure textual description of spatial relationships (used by SpartQA, StepGame) -- rejected because 'spatial relationships can be described in multiple ways, leading to bias,' description granularity is ambiguous, and complex scenes create excessive/imprecise text; (2) pure visual/image representation (used by BabyAI, Minigrid) -- rejected because it depends on the model's visual-processing ability (with its own flaws/hallucination risk) and can be 'overly complex, potentially confusing the agent' (Methods -- Grid Representation to An LLM-based Agent). GRASP's chosen format is a single hybrid: an 11x11 ASCII matrix with axis coordinate labels, cells separated by '+---+' and '|', characters E/O/A/blank, extra whitespace padding for alignment (exact format quoted above). No ablation across alternative representations (e.g., adjacency list, coordinate list) is run in this paper -- only this one grid-text format is used for all LLM experiments; the paper only discusses the representation choice qualitatively, not empirically (no accuracy comparison across formats is reported in Table 1 or elsewhere in the extraction).

models.name: GPT-3.5-Turbo (gpt-3.5-turbo-0125)
strategies: full-grid single-call planning, no CoT/few-shot/ReAct reported
models.key_results: Worst of the 3 LLMs in 14 of 14 control settings (Results -- Performance Evaluation); average Energy = -2.02 (Table 1, Average row) vs greedy search's +0.35 and random walk's -1.14; average path length ~19.4 steps (Table 1). Underperforms the random-walk baseline overall.

models.name: GPT-4o
models.key_results: Average Energy = -1.21 (Table 1, Average row), roughly on par with random walk (unpaired t-test t(17598) = -0.48, p = 0.632, Results section) but worse than greedy search (p < 0.001) and worse than GPT-o1-mini (t(2398) = -6.27, p < 0.001). Better than GPT-3.5-Turbo (t(3198) = 6.78, p < 0.001). Shortest average path length among LLMs, 16-17 steps (Table 1; Results prose). Performs best without obstacles (t(1598)=3.03, p<0.01) and without energy-carrying limit (t(1598)=4.51, p<0.001); best Energy distribution setting is 'random' (Results, Performance Evaluation).

models.name: GPT-o1-mini
strategies: zero-shot only, default temperature=1 (fixed, cannot be changed)
all instructions/grid folded into user prompt only (no system prompt support)
evaluated on a smaller subset: grid instances 0-4 only, vs 0-9 for the other two models
models.key_results: Best-performing LLM: average Energy = -0.31 (Table 1, Average row), significantly better than random walk (t(16798) = -7.64, p < 0.001) and better than GPT-4o (t(2398) = -6.27, p < 0.001), but still worse than greedy search (p < 0.001). Average path length ~18.5 steps (Table 1). Best Energy-distribution setting is 'spiral' but difference not statistically significant (p > 0.01, paired t-tests). Performs best without energy-carrying limit, difference of 1.11 energy units (t(798) = 4.62, p < 0.001).

headline_numbers: Greedy-search baseline achieves the best average Energy (+0.35) across all 14 settings and outperforms all 3 LLMs (p<0.001 for all) -- Table 1, Average row; Results -- Performance Evaluation.
GPT-3.5-Turbo underperforms random walk overall (worst LLM in 14/14 settings; average Energy -2.02 vs random walk's -1.14) -- Table 1; Results section.
GPT-4o is statistically indistinguishable from random walk overall (t(17598) = -0.48, p = 0.632) despite being a much more capable model -- Results, Performance Evaluation.
GPT-o1-mini is the only LLM to beat random walk (t(16798) = -7.64, p < 0.001), average Energy -0.31, but still loses to greedy search -- Table 1; Discussion.

Energy-cost-per-step constraint (0.3 units/step) causes the single largest performance drop for every LLM tested, minimum difference 5.1 energy units for GPT-4o (p<0.001 for all 3 LLMs) -- Results -- Performance Evaluation, paragraph beginning 'All three LLM-based agents achieve notably lesser energy...'.

Movement-action-set (4-dir vs 8-dir) and starting-position (inner vs outer) controls have the least effect on LLM performance, max difference <0.26 energy units, p>0.1 for all LLMs -- Results -- Performance Evaluation.
Benchmark scale: 2,000 grid instances x 8 constraint combinations = 16,000 total environments, on an 11x11 (121-cell) grid, 20-step budget -- Methods -- Grid Environments / Agent Constraints.

Experiments used only a 1/10 subset of the full benchmark (grid indices 0-9) for GPT-3.5-Turbo/GPT-4o, and an even smaller subset (indices 0-4) for GPT-o1-mini -- Results -- Experimental Setup and Parameters.
failure_modes: GPT-3.5-Turbo: includes energy cells in its path but often fails to actually collect the energy; repeatedly attempts to move through obstacle cells; does not return to the starting cell to drop collected energy (Results -- Performance Evaluation, Figure 2 discussion).

GPT-4o: 'inefficient behaviors, such as missing nearby energy cells... and taking extra and unnecessary steps' despite otherwise avoiding obstacles and moving toward energy (Results, Figure 2 discussion).
GPT-o1-mini: 'sometimes overlooked nearby cells with energy or took longer paths than necessary' -- best of the three but still suboptimal vs greedy search (Results; Discussion).

Six documented GPT-4o negative-result categories per the supp2.png composite caption (Supplementary Material, Figure 4), illustrated by seven individual temp*.png captions in the source (temp1.png-temp7.png; category (c) 'not utilizing all available steps' has two separate worked examples, temp6.png and temp7.png): (a) fails to avoid an obstacle; (b) takes energy from a distant cell instead of a nearby one; (c) does not utilize all available steps; (d) prefers a distant energy cell over a near one even under a per-step energy cost; (e) attempts to move beyond the grid boundary (invalid action); (f) repeatedly oscillates back and forth between the same cells (e.g., action sequence [RIGHT,TAKE,LEFT,DROP,UPLEFT,TAKE,DOWNRIGHT,DROP,...] looping between two cell pairs, quoted verbatim under temp1.png caption).

Looping/oscillation and boundary-violation failures are called out explicitly as reproducible action-sequence artifacts (temp1.png, temp2.png captions), directly analogous to the 'looping' and 'off-by-one/edge' failure classes relevant to grid-navigation evaluation generally.
one_shot_vs_multistep: GRASP is architected specifically to AVOID an interactive/multi-turn loop: the agent is given the entire grid, all constraints, and the full task specification upfront in a single prompt, and must emit the ENTIRE action sequence (up to 20 steps) in one response, with 'no further interaction with the environment' (Results -- Experimental Setup and Parameters). The Discussion section states this explicitly as a design choice versus other benchmarks: 'the agent begins with complete knowledge of the grid's layout and all necessary task information... our framework provides all information upfront, eliminating the need for sequential interaction with the environment' (Discussion, second paragraph). The paper's conclusion is that even under this simplified one-shot-plan regime (no exploration, no partial observability, no interactive replanning needed), current LLMs still cannot reliably produce good MULTI-STEP plans over a fully-specified grid: they underperform even a naive greedy one-step-lookahead baseline. The paper does NOT test or report on single derived-fact prediction (e.g., final coordinate only) as a separate, easier sub-task -- the entire evaluation targets full trajectory generation, so no direct evidence exists in-paper about whether one-forward-pass STATE prediction (as opposed to plan generation) would be easier. This distinction is left unaddressed in the paper (Discussion -- limitations only mention zero-shot-only evaluation as a limitation, not single-fact vs. multi-step framing).

relation_to_candidate.quadrant: Full-observation x single-shot-call (but multi-step OUTPUT): GRASP sits in the 'full observation' column since the entire grid/constraints are given upfront in one prompt with no partial observability or exploration required. On the protocol axis it is single-shot at the API-call level (one LLM call, no interaction loop) but its predicted OUTPUT is a full multi-step action plan/trajectory (up to 20 actions), not a single derived fact -- so it differs from c19's single-shot + single-derived-fact design even though both are single-call, full-observation setups.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both use small ASCII/text grid renderings with coordinate labels as the primary representation (GRASP: 11x11 matrix with axis labels 0-10, dashed borders, E/O/A characters; c19: ~6x6 grid with walls and robot pose in ASCII). Both are single LLM-call, fully-observed designs with an independent ground-truth oracle/simulator used for scoring, and both note that off-by-one/edge/obstacle-adjacent errors are diagnostic failure signatures. Both are motivated by the same critique of prior spatial-reasoning benchmarks -- that textual-description QA benchmarks (SpartQA, StepGame) test interpretation rather than the practical use of spatial information -- and both position themselves as more 'practical'/commonsense spatial-reasoning tests than description-based QA.
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Output target: GRASP's model must emit a full multi-step action sequence (a plan) scored by simulating that entire plan and measuring energy collected/steps taken, a continuous-ish multi-dimensional score; c19 asks for exactly ONE derived fact (final coordinate/heading/carrying-flag) scored by strict exact match against an oracle -- this removes GRASP's many-valid-plans ambiguity entirely. (2) Task semantics: GRASP is a resource-collection/foraging-and-return optimization problem (not pure state tracking) with a genuine planning/optimization objective (maximize energy net of step and carry-limit costs); c19 is pure deterministic state tracking under a stated small rule set (moves + 2-3 invented commands), with no optimization objective at all -- just correct simulation. (3) Grid scale: GRASP uses fixed 11x11 (121-cell) grids across all 16,000 instances; c19 uses much smaller ~6x6 grids. (4) Rule novelty: GRASP's action set is fixed/standard (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT(+diagonals), TAKE, DROP) and constraint variation is about world parameters (obstacles, carry-limit, cost-per-step), not about redefining movement semantics; c19 deliberately introduces invented commands and varies wrap-vs-clamp/turn-relative conventions specifically to defeat memorized/fixed-convention answers, which GRASP does not attempt. (5) Model roster/recency: GRASP tests GPT-3.5-Turbo/GPT-4o/GPT-o1-mini (2024 models) with zero-shot only; c19 is being designed for current-generation testing.
relation_to_candidate.what_c19_would_add: c19 would add: (a) an exact-match, single-token scoring target that removes the multi-valid-plan ambiguity inherent to GRASP's trajectory-based scoring, making pass/fail unambiguous and easier to diagnose per-rule; (b) deliberately invented/non-standard command semantics and wrap-vs-clamp/indexing convention variation specifically engineered to prevent a single fixed system-prompt convention statement from covering the whole test pool -- GRASP's action set is entirely conventional and does not test this; (c) a much smaller grid (~6x6 vs 11x11) trading environment richness for tighter, more surgical diagnosis of exactly which rule a model gets wrong (per GRASP's own admission, its 11x11/20-step full-trajectory setup makes it hard to localize exactly where in a bad plan the model's spatial reasoning failed); (d) a state-tracking framing (aligned with bAbI/EntNet/entity-tracking lineage) rather than a resource-optimization/foraging framing, isolating deterministic simulation ability from planning/optimization ability, which GRASP conflates.

corpus_claim: GRASP provides sixteen-thousand grid-based commonsense spatial-reasoning environments and is the direct grid-world antecedent of c19.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. The '16,000 grid-based environments' figure is directly and exactly verified: Abstract states 'a large-scale benchmark called GRASP, which consists of 16,000 grid-based environments,' and Methods confirms the arithmetic (2,000 grid instances x 2x2x2 = 16,000 combinations, Methods -- Agent Constraints). The 'commonsense spatial reasoning' framing is also directly verified -- it is the paper's own named construct (title, abstract, throughout). However, calling GRASP the 'direct grid-world antecedent of c19' overstates the structural similarity: GRASP is fundamentally a multi-step PLAN-GENERATION / resource-collection-and-optimization benchmark (predict a full 20-step action sequence, scored on energy collected net of costs), not a single-derived-fact STATE-PREDICTION benchmark like c19. GRASP shares c19's full-observation, single-LLM-call, ASCII-grid-with-coordinates design and its motivating critique of description-based spatial QA benchmarks, but it does not test or report state-prediction-after-a-fixed-command-sequence as a task in its own right, and it has no invented-command/wrap-vs-clamp convention-manipulation design. The corpus dossier for c19 itself (c19.md) treats GRASP as one of four 'literature parents' alongside GridRoute, LLM-BabyBench, and bAbI -- with LLM-BabyBench's 'Predict split' described as 'the c19 template,' not GRASP. So GRASP is more accurately a grid-world commonsense-reasoning antecedent/relative in the same family, not the specific state-prediction antecedent; the 'direct... antecedent' language should be softened or attributed instead primarily to LLM-BabyBench/bAbI.
baseline_relevant: Grid size used: fixed 11x11 (121 cells) for all 16,000 instances (Methods -- Grid Environments) -- notably much larger than c19's proposed ~6x6 grids; useful reference point for how much larger scale drove poor LLM performance even under full observation.
Step budget: fixed at 20 steps per instance (Methods -- Agent Constraints) regardless of grid complexity.

Prompt format that mattered: the paper explicitly argues (Methods -- Grid Representation) that a hybrid coordinate-labeled ASCII matrix (with '+---+'/'|' cell borders and single-character E/O/A tokens) balances the biases of pure-text description vs. the complexity/hallucination risk of visual/image input -- but this is argued qualitatively, not tested against alternative formats within this paper (no ablation reported).

Metrics used: 'Length' (average steps taken) and 'Energy' (average net energy collected and successfully dropped at start, after costs) per control setting (Table 1); statistical comparisons via unpaired/paired t-tests across control settings.

Floors/ceilings observed: random walk baseline Energy = -1.14 average (a genuine floor, since it is uninformed); greedy search baseline Energy = +0.35 average (an attainable ceiling with zero planning/lookahead, i.e., a 'floor for competence' that all three tested LLMs fail to reach); best LLM (GPT-o1-mini) only reaches -0.31, still well below greedy search's +0.35 -- suggesting substantial headroom remains between LLM performance and even simple non-learned heuristics.

Data/contamination status: full benchmark and code publicly released on GitHub (https://github.com/jasontangzs0/GRASP, per Data Availability section) as of the paper's writing -- meaning GRASP itself carries contamination risk for any model trained/fine-tuned after its release; the paper's own experiments used only small index subsets (0-9, or 0-4 for GPT-o1-mini) of the full 16,000, i.e., only ~0.06%-0.03% of the benchmark was actually evaluated per model, a useful caution about the gap between 'benchmark size' and 'instances actually run.'

Zero-shot only: the paper explicitly flags as a limitation that 'our evaluation was limited to zero-shot prompting methods for LLMs, potentially underestimating their true capabilities' (Discussion -- limitations) -- relevant for baseline design decisions about whether to test CoT/few-shot variants.

Constraint-driven performance swings: energy-cost-per-step constraint caused the single largest LLM performance degradation observed (min. 5.1 energy units for GPT-4o, p<0.001 for all three LLMs) -- suggests that adding cost/penalty constraints is a strong lever for stressing models beyond mere navigation correctness, distinct from raw grid-reasoning difficulty.
quotes: "we construct a large-scale benchmark called GRASP, which consists of 16,000 grid-based environments where the agent is tasked with an energy collection problem" (Abstract).

"These models are tested using zero-shot prompting... the grid is provided in the user prompt; the LLMs are required to provide a full list of actions in the response, with no further interaction with the environment." (Results -- Experimental Setup and Parameters).

"Moreover, in our grid environment, the agent begins with complete knowledge of the grid's layout and all necessary task information... our framework provides all information upfront, eliminating the need for sequential interaction with the environment." (Discussion, second paragraph).

"We present the grid as an 11 by 11 matrix labeled with coordinates from 0 to 10 on both the x-axis (top) and y-axis (left)... a unit of energy is represented as 'E'; an obstacle is represented as 'O'; the agent is represented as 'A'." (Methods -- Grid Representation to An LLM-based Agent).

"GPT-3.5-Turbo performs the worst in 14 out of 14 settings... GPT-4o performs better than GPT-3.5-Turbo... but worse than GPT-o1-mini... and is roughly on par with the random walk agent (t(17598) = -0.48, p = 0.632). All LLM-based agents perform worse than the greedy search agent (p < 0.001), with only GPT-o1-mini performing better than the random walk method" (Results -- Performance Evaluation).

"All three LLM-based agents achieve notably lesser energy when given the constraint regarding the energy cost per step than when given no such constraint, with the minimum difference being 5.1 units of energy from GPT-4o (p < 0.001 for all LLM-based agents)." (Results -- Performance Evaluation).

"we find that GPT-3.5-Turbo includes cells with energy in its path but often fails to collect energy, repeatedly attempts to move through the cell with an obstacle, and does not return to the starting cell to drop the collected energy." (Results -- Performance Evaluation, Figure 2 discussion).
"Finally, our evaluation was limited to zero-shot prompting methods for LLMs, potentially underestimating their true capabilities." (Discussion -- limitations).

corrections: Section attribution error (x2): protocol.description and one_shot_vs_multistep both cited the 'no further interaction with the environment' quote as '(Methods, Experimental Setup and Parameters)' / '(Methods -- Experimental Setup and Parameters)'. 'Experimental Setup and Parameters' is actually a subsection of Results, not Methods, in the source (confirmed via section-header scan of the paper file). Fixed both to 'Results -- Experimental Setup and Parameters', consistent with the correct attribution already used elsewhere in the same dossier (e.g. in the quotes array).

failure_modes entry undercounted the supplementary negative-result images: dossier said 'six documented GPT-4o negative-result cases... + six temp*.png captions', but the source file contains seven individual temp*.png captions (temp1.png-temp7.png), even though the composite supp2.png figure caption only lists six lettered categories (a)-(f). Category (c) 'not utilizing all available steps' is illustrated by two separate examples, temp6.png and temp7.png. Corrected the entry to state six caption categories but seven individual temp*.png worked examples, with the temp6/temp7 duplication noted explicitly.
